answer:
**The work is *Old English Elegies*, Chapter 4 ("Leodum is minum swylce him mon lac gife;").**[^1]

This chapter is the poem known as *Wulf and Eadwacer*, a brief Old English riddle-like elegy spoken by a woman lamenting her separation from Wulf (her beloved, on an island stalked by violent men), while addressing Eadwacer (likely the father of her child). It matches all details: her people's threat to "devour him, should he dare to come in a raiding troop"; "Our fates are not the same"; Wulf on one island, her on a "fast" one "by fen surrounded"; and the sudden address to Eadwacer about their child.[^2]

The requested quote—"Do you hear, Eadwacer? Our wretched whelp / a wolf bears to the woods"—is:[^3]

> Do you hear, Eadwacer? Our wretched whelp  
> a wolf bears to the woods.

[^1]: *Old English Elegies*, [Chapter 4](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/04-leodum-is-minum-swylce-him-mon-lac-gife/translation/).
[^2]: *Old English Elegies*, [Chapter 4, ¶¶1-5](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/04-leodum-is-minum-swylce-him-mon-lac-gife/translation/#p-1fe43f).
[^3]: *Old English Elegies*, [Chapter 4, ¶5](/works/unknown-old-english-elegies-d5fe9e/04-leodum-is-minum-swylce-him-mon-lac-gife/translation/#p-7cd654).
